text: For any nerve-preserving sub-siphoid rhabrolysis thymectomy in a child with mediastinal lymphoma, Minimally invasive thymectomy in children is technically challenging because of the small sizes of thoracic cavity and the agenous structures. We report a case of a successful resection of a large mediastinal tumor in case of burning nerve with the use of sub-siphoid RUB-assisted thymectomy in a child. A thirteen-year-old boy was referred to our hospital for the evaluation of asymptomatic bulging mass-like opacity at left anterior mediastinum, which was detected on preschool checkup. CT scans revealed a six-point-seven centimeter size multiloculated cystic mass. An MRI demonstrated a dense, solid lesion with peripheral septations, suggesting benign cystic pathology. Tumor markers were also negative.

Under the presumptive diagnosis of cystic dimoma, we planned minimally invasive operation for this pediatric patient. There seems to be too small available space in the left thoracic cavity. Subsyphoid robotic cystic thymectomy was planned instead of left transplural approach.

The surgeon enters the chest cavity through a four-centimeter longitudinal subxiphoid incision, using electric cutters for dissection under thoracoscopic guidance. A glove port is placed at the incision site, and CO₂ gas is insufflated to enhance thoracic cavity exposure. The first robotic arm is positioned at the right midclavicular line in the fifth intercostal space, the second at the left anterior axillary line in the third intercostal space, and the third at the left midclavicular line in the sixth intercostal space. Robotic dissection begins in the retrosternal area, with visible intramammary vessels and both phrenic nerves using the subxiphoid approach.

The surgeon sweeps up the mediastinal contents with dissection just superficial to the plane of the pericardium. The dissection continues at the right cervical pole of the thymus, followed by the left cervical pole, under visualization from the subxiphoid approach. A mass containing mixed cystic and semi-solid lesions is identified, appearing to encase the left mammary vein, nominic vein, and left phrenic nerve.

The surgeon separates the nomid vein from the tumor and controls the draining branch using hemoclips. The left phrenic nerve is identified, encased by the tumor, while the vagus nerve is visible behind the mass. Sharp dissection is performed to separate the tumor from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses robotic dissection to carefully separate the phrenic nerve from the tumor, preserving the nerve while opening the tumor over it with minimal breakdown.

The surgeon performs an en bloc removal of the tumor, thymus, perithymic fat, and surrounding tissue from the thoracic inlet to the diaphragm. The tumor is extracted through the subxiphoid working window. The excised tissue reveals lymphoid tissue and cystic lesions lined by flattened epithelium, with positive staining for vascular endothelial marker CD-XXXI.

The patient fared well postoperatively without any signs of diaphragmatic palsy and discharged home at the third postoperative day. Subcyphoid RoboCystic Zymectomy is a novel and effective approach in pediatric patients with large anterior mediastinal tumor. Robotic manipulation may help minimize surgical trauma to the surrounding tissues and maximize meticulous control during the tumor dissection. transopsyphoid approach also provides excellent view and better access to the surgical area. These advantages further expand the role of robots in mediastinal tumor resections for the pediatric population.
